back_translation: Elisha said, “Hear the word of the LORD! Thus says the LORD: ‘About this time tomorrow, at the gate of Samaria, a seah of fine flour will sell for a shekel and two seahs of barley for a shekel.’”

But the officer on whose arm the king leaned answered the man of God, “Look, even if the LORD were to make windows in heaven, could this happen?” Elisha said, “You will see it with your own eyes, but you will not eat any of it.”
Now there were four men with leprosy at the entrance of the city gate, and they said to one another, “Why should we sit here until we die?

If we say, ‘Let us enter the city,’ we will die there from the famine in the city; and if we sit here, we will die too. So come, let us go over to the camp of the Arameans. If they spare us, we live; if they kill us, we die.”
So they arose at twilight and went to the camp of the Arameans; but when they reached the edge of the camp, there was no one there.

For the Lord had made the army of Aram hear the sound of chariots, the sound of horses, the sound of a great army, so they said to one another, “Look, the king of Israel has hired the kings of the Hittites and the kings of Egypt to attack us.”
So they arose and fled at twilight, abandoning their tents, horses, and donkeys; they left the camp as it was and fled for their lives.

When the lepers reached the edge of the camp, they went into a tent and ate and drank, then carried off silver, gold, and clothing and hid them; they came back and entered another tent, carried off items, and hid them.

Then they said to one another, “We are not doing right. Today is a day of good news. If we stay silent and wait until morning light, our guilt will overtake us. So come, let us go and tell the king’s household.”

So they went and called to the gatekeepers of the city, saying, “We went to the camp of the Arameans, and there was no one there—not even a human sound—only tethered horses and donkeys, and the tents left as they were.”
The gatekeepers shouted out the news, and it was reported to the king’s household.

The king rose in the night and said to his servants, “Let me tell you what the Arameans have done to us. They know we are starving, so they have left the camp to hide in the field, thinking, ‘When they come out of the city, we will take them alive and enter the city.’”

One of his servants said, “Please let some men take five of the horses that remain in the city—they will fare no worse than all the Israelites left here who are about to perish—and let us send them to find out.”
So they took two chariots with horses, and the king sent them after the army of Aram, saying, “Go and see.”
They followed them as far as the Jordan, and the whole way was strewn with clothing and equipment that the Arameans had thrown away in their haste. So the messengers returned and told the king.
Then the people went out and plundered the camp of the Arameans, and a seah of fine flour sold for a shekel and two seahs of barley for a shekel, according to the word of the LORD.
Now the king had put the officer on whose arm he leaned in charge of the gate, but the people trampled him at the gate, and he died, just as the man of God had said when the king came down to him.
It happened just as the man of God had told the king: “About this time tomorrow, at the gate of Samaria, two seahs of barley will sell for a shekel and a seah of fine flour for a shekel.”

The officer had answered the man of God, “Look, even if the LORD were to make windows in heaven, could this happen?” and Elisha had said, “You will see it with your own eyes, but you will not eat any of it.”
And so it happened to him: the people trampled him at the gate, and he died.
